reflection.quote: The most regretful people on earth are those who felt the call to creative work, who felt their own creative power restive and uprising, and gave to it neither power nor time.
reflection.reading:
Sundays used to be the worst day of the week. Not because anything happened on Sundays, but because nothing did. There was too much quiet, too much time to sit inside my own head, too much space between me and the next thing that would blur the edges. I'd fill it with whatever was around — a hangover to nurse, a bottle to open early, plans I'd cancel by 4pm. The day would drag itself toward Monday and I'd be relieved when it did.

Mary Oliver was writing about creative work, but she could have been writing about any of it — the life restive and uprising inside you, waiting to be given power and time. That's what an ordinary Sunday actually is. Not a void to escape. A canvas nobody's watching you paint. You get to decide if it's a run at sunrise, a long breakfast with someone you love, a book you finally finish, a hike that leaves your legs shaking. Or nothing at all — just being awake for it.

Eleven years in, Sundays are my favorite day. Not because they're exciting, but because I'm actually here for them. That's the trade. You stop numbing the quiet, and eventually the quiet stops being something to survive.
reflection.meditation: What would I do with today if I trusted myself to remember it?

affirmations: I am present for the whole day, not just the parts that entertain me.
Today I trust the quiet instead of trying to fill it.
I am building a life I don't need to escape from.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful today for a Sunday I get to be awake for. For coffee that tastes like coffee. For the people who text me back, the body that carries me through the day, and the plain fact of not waking up in the wreckage of last night. Eleven years ago I couldn't have imagined this being enough. It's more than enough.
gratitude.closing: One more day, given freely. I'll try to be worthy of it.
